perturbation.original_text: Philips shall maintain a delivery capacity, which allows Philips to deliver the Products in accordance with the Lead Times, Forecasts and Purchase Orders, but always subject to the supply limitations, if any, outlined in the Production Plan. Such Production Plan may be updated, and the production capacity may be increased only by the Parties' written agreement and any reasonable additional incremental (and documented) investment required to exclusively satisfy such increase shall be borne by Customer. Philips shall not be held liable for rejecting any Purchase Order through which the volume in any Contract Year or the relevant quarter exceeds the volume indicated in the Production Plan.
perturbation.changed_text: Philips shall maintain a delivery capacity to fulfill Purchase Orders. Philips shall not be held liable for rejecting any Purchase Order.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the clause 'which allows Philips to deliver the Products in accordance with the Lead Times, Forecasts and Purchase Orders, but always subject to the supply limitations, if any, outlined in the Production Plan. Such Production Plan may be updated, and the production capacity may be increased only by the Parties' written agreement and any reasonable additional incremental (and documented) investment required to exclusively satisfy such increase shall be borne by Customer.' creates an in-text contradiction because the contract later refers to 'Lead-Time', 'Forecasts', and 'Production Plan'. By deleting this clause, the explicit connection between Philips's delivery capacity and these defined terms is removed, creating uncertainty about their relevance and enforceability in relation to delivery obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Customer shall place Purchase Orders in writing (or any other mutually agreed manner of communication, e.g. email or EDI) within the Lead-Time and in accordance with the Forecast provided to Philips in accordance with clause 3.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit link between Philips' delivery capacity and key terms like 'Lead-Time', 'Forecasts', and 'Production Plan'. The contradicted text states, 'Customer shall place Purchase Orders in writing (or any other mutually agreed manner of communication, e.g. email or EDI) within the Lead-Time and in accordance with the Forecast provided to Philips in accordance with clause 3.' By removing the references to Lead-Time, Forecasts, and Production Plan in the changed text (which originally specified Philips' delivery obligations in relation to these terms), the contract now lacks clarity on how these terms relate to Philips's delivery obligations. This creates a contradiction because the Customer is still obligated to place orders based on Lead-Time and Forecasts, but the changed text does not clearly define how Philips will use these terms in relation to its delivery capacity. As such, the Omissions – In Text Contradiction definition is satisfied since 'essential language is removed or altered in one section, creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.'

perturbation.original_text: Customer and Philips agree to notify each other within two (2) Business Days of any regulatory action affecting the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer and Philips agree to collaborate on product issues.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the specific notification requirement for regulatory actions to a vague 'collaboration on product issues' creates an internal contradiction because the contract still references the Quality Agreement and compliance with laws, which inherently depend on timely notification of regulatory actions. Without a defined notification process, compliance becomes ambiguous, especially given the Regulatory Transfer Date is mentioned later in the document on clauses 8.2 and 17.2. By making this change, it can create uncertainties of the terms of the Regulatory Transfer Date being moved to Customer in Section 2.3
perturbation.contradicted_text: Until, on a jurisdiction by jurisdiction basis, the Regulatory Transfer Date, Philips shall (a) remain the legal manufacturer of the Product according to its own quality management systems. (b) comply, in all material respects, with all applicable Laws, Philips' operation of its business and the exercise of its rights and performance of its obligations hereunder (including, the manufacture of the Product). Without limitation of the foregoing, Philips shall ensure the Product is manufactured in accordance with applicable Laws. (c) obtain and maintain all Permits necessary for the exercise of its rights and performance of Philips' obligations under this Agreement, including any Permits required for the manufacture of the Product and the import or any materials and other manufacturing parts used in the production and manufacture of the Product, and the shipment of hazardous materials, as applicable. For purposes of this Agreement, "Permit" means any permits, licenses, franchises, approvals, authorizations, registrations, certificates, variances and similar rights obtained or required to be obtained, from any Governmental Authority.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated a specific notification process (within 2 business days) regarding regulatory actions. The changed text replaces this with a vague requirement to 'collaborate on product issues.' This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text explicitly states Philips' obligations to comply with all applicable laws until the Regulatory Transfer Date, which is dependent on timely notification of regulatory actions. Without the specific notification process outlined in the original text, Philips' ability to 'comply, in all material respects, with all applicable Laws' is compromised, creating a direct conflict and inconsistency. Therefore, the change contradicts the clause requiring compliance with applicable laws.

perturbation.original_text: All terms and conditions of this Agreement which are intended (whether expressed or not) to survive the duration or termination of this Agreement will so survive, including, for greater certainty, clauses 2.3, 2.4, 6, 10, 12, 13, 14, and 18.
perturbation.changed_text: All terms and conditions of this Agreement which are intended (whether expressed or not) to survive the duration or termination of this Agreement will so survive.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit listing of clauses that survive termination, such as clauses 2.3, 2.4, 6, 10, 12, 13, 14, and 18 creates uncertainty. Clause 2.3 pertains to the Regulatory Transfer Date, meaning that is it not clear which clauses would remain valid after termination. This creates ambiguity regarding obligations following the contract's end, potentially leading to disputes about which responsibilities continue to exist. The lack of specified surviving clauses creates confusion and contradictions in enforcing post-termination obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: On the Effective Date, Philips (or its Affiliate, Philips Oy) is the legal manufacturer of the Product. Customer hereby covenants and agrees to file with all applicable notified bodies and Governmental Authorities, including but not limited to BSI, Notified Body and ISO Registrar, on a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction basis, within the applicable time periods for each jurisdiction outlined in the "Transitional Service Level Agreement" (TSLA Number: QR01, TSLA Title: Transfer of Legal Manufacturers), all documentation required or necessary to change the legal manufacturer of the Product in each Required Jurisdiction from Philips Oy to Customer for all applicable regulatory purposes. In order to expedite and achieve such change of legal manufacturer and to achieve the required changes outlined in such Transitional Service Level Agreement, Customer shall fully cooperate with as required by and actively facilitate the above registration process by the applicable notified bodies and Governmental Authorities in each Required Jurisdiction. On a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction basis, from the date that the legal manufacturer is updated to Customer in such jurisdiction, Philips will supply the Products in such jurisdiction as contract manufacturer of Customer under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly listed clauses that survive termination, including clause 2.3. The changed text removes this explicit listing, creating uncertainty about which clauses remain valid after termination. Clause 2.3 directly relates to transferring legal manufacturer status to the customer. Therefore, the changed text creates a contradiction because it is no longer clear that the customer must still actively facilitate the registration process to change the legal manufacturer (as stated in the contradicted text), since clause 2.3 might not survive termination.
